EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding caused considerable damage to county roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms accompanied a strong cold front and produced strong and damaging winds...along with large hail. These storms developed in a very unstable airmass across the region on the evening of October 1st as a strong upper level storm system exited the southern plains and entered the lower Mississippi valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS storm survey determined that a downburst occurred north of Buckner, Arkansas, and produced widespread wind damage over a concentrated area. Two chicken houses were severely damaged on the north side of County Road 31 and Hwy. 53.  Nine trees were uprooted across Hwy. 53 and a passenger vehicle was struck by one of the fallen trees which trapped the occupants inside.  There were no report of any injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved across the region during the morning hours of October 9th. These storms were prolific wind makers as widespread wind damage was the result across most of the region. The storms intensified over northeast Texas once they encountered a moist and unstable airmass.  Some excessive heavy rainfall was also noted across portions of southwest Arkansas which resulted in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto a vehicle trapping a man inside.  He was injured and taken to a local hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved across the region during the morning hours of October 9th. The storms intensified over northeast Texas once they encountered a moist and unstable airmass.  Some excessive heavy rainfall was also noted across portions of southwest Arkansas which resulted in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and powerlines were downed in the Taylor, Arkansas community.  Many powerlines were downed between the Southern Arkansas University Campus and the town of McNeil as 7 power poles were downed in that location.  In addition, several trees were downed on the Emerson-Taylor School campus where windows were shattered.  Among roads across the county that were blocked included Arkansas 19 south of Walkerville and Arkansas 98 between Emerson and Arkansas 160 east of Taylor.  County Road 61 in the Ebenezer area was blocked by a downed tree with trees also across County Roads 3, 27 and 80.  In Magnolia, a tree blocked South Washington Street near the Bethlehem Missionary Baptist Church, and other trees blocked portions of West University.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread showers and thunderstorms, in association with an upper level low pressure system, resulted in excessive heavy rainfall across much of the region on October 13th. Widespread 2 to 4 inches were reported with isolated amounts in excess of 8 inches were reported. This rainfall led to widespread flash flooding across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several Miller County roads were closed or washed out including CR 19...43 and 215.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved across the region during the morning hours of October 9th. These storms were prolific wind makers as widespread wind damage was the result across most of the region. The storms intensified over northeast Texas once they encountered a moist and unstable airmass.  Some excessive heavy rainfall was also noted across portions of southwest Arkansas which resulted in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed in the community of Junction City, Arkansas blocking four city streets.  Damage was also reported in town where trees were downed at the intersection of North Newton and Oakhurst Boulevard.  Nearly 20 trees were downed within a quarter mile stretch at Ebenezer Road were several structures also received damage.  Two mobile homes were destroyed.  Also, a home was damaged on Smith Street in El Dorado, Arkansas.  Trees also fell on two vehicles across the county and  a canopy was damaged at the Yocum Elementary School.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Excessive heavy rainfall in the northern portion of the Ouachita River Basin resulted in very high water conditions on the Ouachita River at the Felsenthal Lock and Dam.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive heavy rainfall that fell during the middle and latter half of October resulted in very high river levels on the Ouachita River at Felsenthal, Arkansas.  The river did not crest until the first part of November so the crest information will be included in the November publication of Storm Data.  At the end of October, the river level at the Felsenthal Lock and Dam was near 83 feet which was some 13 feet above flood stage. Most of the communities residents had to move to higher ground as water was reported in as many as 15 homes with many city streets flooded and closed.  Upwards of 14 homes in the Calion area were inundated by flood waters.  Shelters were being setup to house the evacuated residents.  Meals were provided by local chapters of the Red Cross.  Most of the towns streets were inundated as well with water 5 foot deep across some roads.  Travel by boat became necessary by the end of the month, especially in the Moro Bay areas.  Union County, Arkansas was declared a Federal Disaster Area because of the extensive flooding and storm damage that occurred during the month of October.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Roads 7, 41, 45, 53, 55, 60, 64, 79, 114, 140, 246 and 349 were all closed due to flooding.  Columbia County was one of 20 counties across the state that were declared disaster areas from rainfall up to the 13th, 14th and 15th of October.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some county roads were closed due to high water.  Union County was one of 20 counties across the state that were declared disaster areas from rainfall up to the 13th, 14th and 15th of October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent complex of thunderstorms raced across the northwest portion of the forecast area and brought a line of severe storms to the Delta region. Several thunderstorms brought damaging winds to portions of southeast Arkansas, northeast Louisiana, and the Delta portion of Mississippi. A couple of tornadoes also occurred during this event with one causing a fatality in Wayside.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were blown down in the area.

Meanwhile, a cold front arrived from the west. Because the system was so far to the north, it did not give the front much push. The front slowed down, which prolonged the rain. The result was widespread flash flooding, which gave way to areal flooding and river flooding. Winds changed direction and speed up through the atmosphere, a favorable condition for tornadoes. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous county roads were damaged by flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved across the region during the morning hours of October 9th. These storms were prolific wind makers as widespread wind damage was the result across most of the region. The storms intensified over northeast Texas once they encountered a moist and unstable airmass.  Some excessive heavy rainfall was also noted across portions of southwest Arkansas which resulted in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Upwards of 20 homes wee damaged across the southern half of Lafayette County, Arkansas from thunderstorms winds.  The homes were damaged from fallen trees.  There were no reports of injuries across the county.  Areas around Lake Erling were especially hit hard as trees and powerlines blocked roads and across to some residential areas.  At least a mile of Hwy. 53 was littered with debris which had to be removed before emergency personnel could assist a motorist trapped in his truck by a tree tangled in high voltage power lines.  One home positioned a the edge of the lake on Laf. 260 had the roof stripped.  Another large tree fell on a truck and the tree also clipped an adjacent home.  Three trees were also downed on mobile homes in the area.  At Sandy Point, it was estimated that three-fourths of the homes had some type of damage.  At Cypress Point, most of the homes were reported to have some type of storm damage ranging from roof damage, downed trees, broken windows, dented walls and damaged vehicles.

Widespread 2 to 4 inches were reported with isolated amounts in excess of 8 inches were reported. This rainfall led to widespread flash flooding across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several bridges were washed out in the Fouke, Arkansas community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread showers and thunderstorms, in association with an upper level low pressure system, resulted in excessive heavy rainfall across much of the region on October 13th. Widespread 2 to 4 inches were reported with isolated amounts in excess of 8 inches were reported. This rainfall led to widespread flash flooding across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hwy. 82 east of Lewisville was closed due to 3 feet of water over the highway.  During the height of the storm, the County Judge said that there were only 3 roads in the county that did not have high water over them.   So many roads were flooded that the county ran out of barricades, high water and street closed signs.  Several cars were flooded and stalled in high water and a woman and child had to be pulled from one car.  Some of the major flooding occurred on highways 313 and 53 as well as County Roads 31, 60, 26, 22, 16 and 18.  Several homes in the Lewisville area were flooded forcing the evacuation of several people.  The rushing water eroded gravel roads, washed out culverts and stranded many residents.  Lafayette County was one of 20 counties across the state that were declared disaster areas from rainfall up to the 13th, 14th and 15th of October.

Widespread 2 to 4 inches were reported with isolated amounts in excess of 8 inches were reported. This rainfall led to widespread flash flooding across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water near two feet was reportedly across Hwy. 71 just north of Ida inside the Miller County Arkansas line but south of the Hwy 549/71 interchange south of Doddgridge.  Hwy. 71 south of Hwy. 549 and north of Ida has been closed.  Up to 30 additional County Roads were damaged from flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large storm system in the Rockies headed through the Plains into the upper Midwest on the 29th. Rain increased ahead of the system, and became widespread in Arkansas. Meanwhile, a cold front arrived from the west. Because the system was so far to the north, it did not give the front much push. The front slowed down, which prolonged the rain. The result was widespread flash flooding, which gave way to areal flooding and river flooding. Winds changed direction and speed up through the atmosphere, a favorable condition for tornadoes. However, instability was sufficient only in southern Arkansas for the development of tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A pickup truck was swept off Webber Chapel Road into an unnamed creek. The driver was killed after being swept downstream. A passenger in the vehicle saved himself by clinging to a tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large storm system in the Rockies headed through the Plains into the upper Midwest on the 29th. Rain increased ahead of the system, and became widespread in Arkansas. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Soon after the tornado touched down, it damaged a church, where the steeple was toppled and the portico blown down. It then took part of the roof off a house. Next, the tornado traveled into a shopping center. A discount store had several doors blown in, part of the facade blown off, and damage to the roof which resulted in water dripping inside the store.. A car, a small pickup truck, and a tractor-trailer were overturned. Shopping carts and cart corrals were blown around the parking lot. An ice machine at a gas station was blown out into the driveway. A couple of restaurants and a branch bank suffered roof damage. A couple of business signs were blown out. Three people were injured in the business area. The tornado then continued into a residential area. One house was badly damaged when a tree fell into it, and a couple of outbuildings were destroyed. Several homes had shingles torn off and a fence was blown down. Along the path of the tornado, dozens of large trees were blown down. One of these trees fell on a car, injuring a person.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread showers and thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a strong cold front as a powerful upper level storm system plowed into the southern plains during the afternoon hours of October 29th. Strong shear was in place across the region such that several tornadic thunderstorms developed...producing widespread wind damage across portions of northeast Texas, southwest Arkansas as well as northern Louisiana. These storms trained across the same general area such that flooding was widespread. Flooding was severe across northwest Louisiana where flooding was reported in many homes and businesses. Area bayous, creeks, rivers and lakes were already at high levels from the excessive heavy rainfall that fell earlier in the month. Thus, some lakes and bayous approached and succeeded their all time record stage levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado touched down 3 miles west southwest of Smackover, Arkansas and downed large trees while snapping several others.  The path width was 75 yards wide with a path length of 1.5 miles.  At least 4 homes received damage from downed trees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large metal, industrial building suffered considerable damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning and afternoon of the 30th, a cold front interacted with a moist and unstable airmass. The rich air mass caused some locally heavy rains across southeast Arkansas. There were closures of roads due to flooded conditions over portions of Ashley and Chicot counties. Numerous roads were closed due to flooding across Ashley county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few roads were flooded around Dermott.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed from the central and southern Plains into the mid-Mississippi Valley early on the 8th. Rain eventually spread into Arkansas later in the day, and was heavy at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low water crossings in Harrison off of U.S. Highway 65 were flooded. Seven water rescues occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A round of precipitation cranked up from the southwest early on the 13th. Rain was especially heavy across the southern half of Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred on several streets in Hampton as well as on U.S. Highways 167 and 278. Flood water entered a couple of apartments, necessitating evacuations during the morning. By evening, nine additional homes were sandbagged, and Arkansas Highways 203 and 172 were blocked by flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system brought heavy rainfall to Arkansas, causing a number of river forecast points in the Little Rock Hydrologic Service Area to exceed flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall during the first part of October pushed the Ouachita River at Arkadelphia above flood stage. Arkansas Highways 7 and 8 were flooded southeast of Arkadelphia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A round of precipitation cranked up from the southwest early on the 13th. Rain was especially heavy across the southern half of Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Countywide flooding occurred and several county roads had to be closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Canadian high pressure shifted to the east of Arkansas, and southerly winds brought a warming trend in the days leading up to October 22nd. Moisture also increased, and rain developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Arkansas Highway 138 and U.S. Highway 278 had water over them, and several county roads were closed due to flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water rescues were carried out south of Salem.

Several thunderstorms brought damaging winds to portions of southeast Arkansas, northeast Louisiana, and the Delta portion of Mississippi. A couple of tornadoes also occurred during this event with one causing a fatality in Wayside.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple trees were blown down on Highway 8 between Hamburg and Parkdale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system wobbled from eastern Texas into southwest Arkansas in mid-September. Precipitation became widespread just to the north and east of the system late on the 15th in west central, central and northeast sections of the state. There was tropical moisture in place, making showers and thunderstorms more efficient rain-makers. Heavy rain continued swirling around the system across Arkansas on the 16th through the 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and unstable air mass spread across the Southern Plains and Ozarks ahead of a strong cold front that moved into northwestern Arkansas during the evening hours of the 8th. Widespread showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the front, which produced locally heavy rainfall amounts of two to five inches with as much as seven inches in portions of northwest Arkansas. A couple of the stronger thunderstorms produced localized damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were flooded and closed in and around Fayetteville, Springdale, and Farmington. Several water rescues were conducted as motorists became stranded in high water. Flood water entered at least 2 homes and also damaged part of an assisted living facility in Farmington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains associated with multiple weather disturbances and abundant tropical moisture brought flooding to the region. Some crops sustained significant damage due to this flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: More heavy rainfall fell across Chicot County on the 13th and caused additional flooding. Many more roads were flooded and closed due to high water. Several homes and businesses were flooded in Dermott and Lake Village. Crops were also heavily damaged by the heavy rain and flooding.

A water rescue was necessary on Pittman Road southeast of DeWitt. The county judge estimated that 90% of county roads had water on them.

In the county, flooded roads included Grant 58, 16, 9, 14, 31, and 313, along with Keg Mill Rd., Fairview Rd., and Arkansas Highway 46 in the Hurricane Creek area. Several homes and businesses suffered flood damage. Two water rescues occurred. The Sheridan schools were closed on the 30th due to flooding on at least 25 of the 44 bus routes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Excessive heavy rainfall in the northern portion of the Ouachita River Basin resulted in very high water conditions on the Ouachita River at the Felsenthal Lock and Dam.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive heavy rainfall that fell during the middle and latter half of October resulted in very high river levels on the Ouachita River at Felsenthal, Arkansas that continued through the entire month of November.  The river crested on November 9th at a stage of 85.78 with flood stage being 70 feet.  The river remained above the 70 foot flood stage through the end of the month and into December.  Most of the communities residents had to move to higher ground as water was reported in as many as 15 homes with many city streets flooded and closed.  Upwards of 14 homes in the Calion area were inundated by flood waters.  Shelters were being setup to house the evacuated residents.  Meals were provided by local chapters of the Red Cross.  Most of the towns streets were inundated as well with water 5 foot deep across some roads.  Travel by boat became necessary by the end of the month, especially in the Moro Bay areas.  Union County, Arkansas was declared a Federal Disaster Area because of the extensive flooding and storm damage that occurred during the month of October and November.  Monetary amounts for the flooding and storm damage that ensued were included in the October Storm Data publication.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A round of precipitation cranked up from the southwest early on the 13th. Rain was especially heavy across the southern half of Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused the roof of an old two-story building on the Courthouse Square in Monticello to collapse. The roof cave-in caused two walls of the building to collapse, sending bricks and other construction materials out into the street and breaking a natural gas line. The broken gas line was beneath the rubble, so repairs were delayed. Nearby businesses were ordered closed until the leak could be repaired.

The result was widespread flash flooding, which gave way to areal flooding and river flooding. Winds changed direction and speed up through the atmosphere, a favorable condition for tornadoes. EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado exited Ouachita County northeast of Frenchport and entered Calhoun County about 6 2/3 miles southwest of Locust Bayou. In Calhoun County, the tornado traveled through wetlands, doing nothing more than knocking down trees. The tornado exited the county about 4 1/3 miles west of Locust Bayou, traveling back into Ouachita County about 2 1/4 miles south-southwest of Lakeside.

This included U.S. Highway 63, and Arkansas Highways 35 and 189. Recent wet weather kept loggers from working, so they used their equipment to help clear some of the roads. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed from the central and southern Plains into the mid-Mississippi Valley early on the 8th. Rain eventually spread into Arkansas later in the day, and was heavy at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were down. Several houses in the area sustained roof damage, and several chicken houses had their roofs torn off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed from the central and southern Plains into the mid-Mississippi Valley early on the 8th. Rain eventually spread into Arkansas later in the day, and was heavy at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few large trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed from the central and southern Plains into the mid-Mississippi Valley early on the 8th. Rain eventually spread into Arkansas later in the day, and was heavy at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed dozens of large trees, power lines and power poles in Warren. Trees fell on several houses causing minor roof damage. The falling trees were also responsible for two gas leaks. Two buildings had much of their metal roofs blown off. Damage was generally in a path from the northwest side of Warren on toward the center of town. One person in Warren was injured when he was hit in the head by flying debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large storm system in the Rockies headed through the Plains into the upper Midwest on the 29th. Rain increased ahead of the system, and became widespread in Arkansas. Meanwhile, a cold front arrived from the west. Because the system was so far to the north, it did not give the front much push. The front slowed down, which prolonged the rain. The result was widespread flash flooding, which gave way to areal flooding and river flooding. Winds changed direction and speed up through the atmosphere, a favorable condition for tornadoes. However, instability was sufficient only in southern Arkansas for the development of tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding damaged some county roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains associated with multiple weather disturbances and abundant tropical moisture brought flooding to the region. Some crops sustained significant damage due to this flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused several roads to flood across western Ashley County along with several other roads across Crossett. Crops across this area were impacted as well with damage due to flooded fields and very wet soils.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado moved out of Union County and into Ouachita County just east-southeast of Louann. Roof damage occurred to a number of residences and the roof was torn off a mobile home. Hundreds of trees were blown down or snapped off, and power lines were blown down. The tornado exited Ouachita County northeast of Frenchport and continued into Calhoun County about 6 2/3 miles southwest of Locust Bayou.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approaches to many low water bridges were destroyed. Bridges on Big Creek and Martin Creek were severely damaged. A bridge on Shaw Cemetery Road was also damaged. Numerous rescues were necessary.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist and unstable air mass spread across the Southern Plains and Ozarks ahead of a strong cold front that moved into northwestern Arkansas during the evening hours of the 8th. Widespread showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the front, which produced locally heavy rainfall amounts of two to five inches with as much as seven inches in portions of northwest Arkansas. A couple of the stronger thunderstorms produced localized damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several county roads and state highways were flooded and closed in and around Huntsville and Hindsville, portions of some were washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms accompanied a strong cold front and produced strong and damaging winds...along with large hail. These storms developed in a very unstable airmass across the region on the evening of October 1st as a strong upper level storm system exited the southern plains and entered the lower Mississippi valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused flash flooding in the Magnolia community.  One car and driver on North Dudley Street near East Main found herself in water up to her car seat.  Two other vehicles flooded on Stadium Drive near the Louisiana and North West Railroad.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large storm system brought heavy rain to Arkansas at the end of October, causing a number of river forecast points in the Little Rock Hydrologic Service Area to exceed flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall at the end of October pushed the White River at Newport above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periodic heavy rainfall allowed river flooding to continue from October into November.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall at the end of October resulted in the Fourche LaFave River near Houston remaining above flood stage into early November.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periodic heavy rainfall allowed river flooding to continue from October into November.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall at the end of October allowed the Petit Jean River at Danville to remain above flood stage into the beginning of November.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall at the end of October allowed the Ouachita River at Camden to remain above flood stage into the middle of November. Some crops suffered damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced severe weather across northwestern Arkansas during the late afternoon through evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Creeks near Japton briefly rose out of banks, flooding several rural roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system developed over Northern Arkansas during the early morning hours of July 16th, 2009 and moved across Eastern Arkansas...Southwest Tennessee and Northwest Mississippi. Some of storms became severe with damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in the vicinity of Osceola. Some back roads became impassable. A couple of county employees were unable to leave their houses in Osceola due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced severe weather across northwestern Arkansas during the late afternoon through evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low lying rural roads were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During May 2009, flooding along the Ouachita River was peaking at levels not experienced in nearly 20 years.  This flooding was coming to an end in early June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The high stages along the Ouachita River that crested in May were receding in early June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms tended to be isolated to scattered on the 29th. All of this precipitation was the result of a relentless northwest wind flow aloft bringing storm systems and fronts into Arkansas from the Plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 52 year-old woman was injured by lightning while selling melons at a roadside stand.  She was holding onto a metal post supporting a tent-like canopy when the lightning struck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of the 30th, a large cluster of thunderstorms known as a Mesoscale Convective System (MCS) moved into Arkansas from the southwest.   The MCS weakened, leaving behind a Mesoscale Convective Vortex (MCV) or small area of circulation aloft.  During the afternoon, this MCV helped to trigger new storms and a few tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portion of Arkansas Highway 53 South was flooded. A gas station on U.S. Highway 67 had to be closed due to minor flooding. A severe washout occurred on Old Gurdon Highway between Gurdon and Beirne, with at least 75% of the road surface washed away on one part of the road. A part of Vaden Road east of Curtis was washed away, as was a bridge on Mount Olive Road. Also, Cedar Grove Road was damaged, as was a bridge on Central Road. Terre Noire and Bell Creeks flooded Davidson Campground, causing people to move their campers and RVs to higher ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved across the region during the morning hours of October 9th. These storms were prolific wind makers as widespread wind damage was the result across most of the region. The storms intensified over northeast Texas once they encountered a moist and unstable airmass.  Some excessive heavy rainfall was also noted across portions of southwest Arkansas which resulted in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and powerlines were downed in the Liberty Eulau community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periodic heavy rainfall during October caused a number of reporting stations in Arkansas to set new rainfall records for the month.  At three stations, October was the wettest month ever recorded.  At two stations, rainfall in October pushed the stations' yearly rainfall total so high such that the towns were assured that 2009 will be the wettest year ever recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In North Little Rock, October was the wettest month ever recorded.  In addition, October's rainfall caused North Little Rock to break the record for the wettest year ever recorded in the city. Rainfall records began in 1976.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Monticello, it was the wettest October ever recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved slowly through the Mid-South during the time period of September 14th-September 16th, 2009. Deep moisture associated with the upper low produced several rounds of heavy rain causing flash flooding over much of the Mid-South.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding west of Aubrey. County Road 157 was flooded as a result.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One large, old oak tree was uprooted and another was snapped off. Shingles were blown off a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front moved through the region starting on the evening of October 29th and exited the region by late afternoon on October 30th. Numerous showers and thunderstorms produced flash flooding across portions of east Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in Cross County. Numerous roads were closed as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front was located across southern portions of the Midsouth during the overnight hours of May 5th continuing into May 6, 2009. Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed along the front. Some of the thunderstorms were severe and produced damaging winds, isolated tornadoes, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds blew numerous trees and power lines down in and around Helena-West Helena.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front along the Gulf Coast began lifting slowly to the north. This resulted in a slight warm up (temperatures climbed into the 60s), and also triggered scattered thunderstorms in western Arkansas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Arkansas Highway 10 was flooded between Booneville and the Magazine Bridge. Damage to bridges occurred at Marcelle Phillips Park and Veterans Park in Booneville. An aluminum bridge on Earl Prairie Rd. was washed out. Part of Brewer Rd. washed away and a pipe washed out on Goff Rd. Water rose to a depth of more than 3 feet at a feed store in Booneville, causing merchandise to float around in the store. A hardware store also suffered flood damage. Numerous county roads were flooded, especially in the southern half of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large storm system in the Rockies headed through the Plains into the upper Midwest on the 29th. Rain increased ahead of the system, and became widespread in Arkansas. Meanwhile, a cold front arrived from the west. Because the system was so far to the north, it did not give the front much push. The front slowed down, which prolonged the rain. The result was widespread flash flooding, which gave way to areal flooding and river flooding. Winds changed direction and speed up through the atmosphere, a favorable condition for tornadoes. However, instability was sufficient only in southern Arkansas for the development of tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado exited the Ouachita County portion of East Camden and moved into the Calhoun County portion of the town and the Highland Industrial Park. At Southern Arkansas University Tech, a number of buildings suffered roof damage, and several 18-wheeler trailers were blown over. Many trees were blown down, some of which fell on vehicles parked at the university.. The tornado then exited Calhoun County and once again moved into Ouachita County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms producing wind damage spread from eastern Oklahoma into northwest and west central Arkansas during the early evening hours of the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 60 mile an hour wind gust from a thunderstorm was reported by amateur radio.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periodic heavy rainfall during October caused a number of reporting stations in Arkansas to set new rainfall records for the month.  At three stations, October was the wettest month ever recorded.  At two stations, rainfall in October pushed the stations' yearly rainfall total so high such that the towns were assured that 2009 will be the wettest year ever recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Camden, October was the wettest month ever recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By the afternoon of May 9th through May 10th, the atmosphere became very unstable across the region and storms produced large hail and damaging thunderstorm wind gusts across northeast Texas, southeast Oklahoma and southwest Arkansas. Locally heavy rainfall also produced flash flooding across much of the region as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hwy. 71 near the paper mill was flooded and closed.  Heavy rainfall plummeted through the roof of the planned Two Rivers Museum annex, soaking museum artifacts and exhibits.  The Central Baptist Church and several other homes sustained water damage from the high waters that clogged city drainage ditches.  Rainwater also backed up into a mobile home park near the Arkansas 32 Bypass causing some flooding concerns.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ridge of high pressure began building into Arkansas from the Plains on the 11th. Around the periphery of the high, a large cluster of thunderstorms formed during the evening in southern Missouri and moved into Arkansas. The storms tracked through the northern and eastern counties through pre-dawn hours of the 12th. The storms weakened toward dawn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A hay barn was blown down, the roof was torn off a building, and trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex developed over Oklahoma during the early morning hours of June 15th, 2009. The complex moved east along a stationary front eventually moving into the Mid-South during the day continuing into the early evening hours on June 15th, 2009. Many storms became severe producing large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were estimated at 60 mph in Maynard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periodic heavy rainfall during July caused a number of reporting stations in Arkansas to set new rainfall records for the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: North Little Rock broke the record for the greatest rainfall ever recorded in the month of July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved across the region during the morning hours of October 9th. These storms were prolific wind makers as widespread wind damage was the result across most of the region. The storms intensified over northeast Texas once they encountered a moist and unstable airmass.  Some excessive heavy rainfall was also noted across portions of southwest Arkansas which resulted in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Road 73 west northwest of Hope, Arkansas was closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread showers and thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a strong cold front as a powerful upper level storm system plowed into the southern plains during the afternoon hours of October 29th. Strong shear was in place across the region such that several tornadic thunderstorms developed...producing widespread wind damage across portions of northeast Texas, southwest Arkansas as well as northern Louisiana. These storms trained across the same general area such that flooding was widespread. Flooding was severe across northwest Louisiana where flooding was reported in many homes and businesses. Area bayous, creeks, rivers and lakes were already at high levels from the excessive heavy rainfall that fell earlier in the month. Thus, some lakes and bayous approached and succeeded their all time record stage levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Another EF1 tornado touched down on the northeast side of Magnolia, Arkansas.  The tornado touched down on CR 47 about one quarter of a mile east of Hwy. 79.  Some significant to major damage was observed to several mobile homes and powerlines and poles were snapped.  A residential shop was also damaged by fallen trees. Winds were estimated at 95-105 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Returning moisture overran a relative cool surface during the late evening hours of April 11th into the predawn hours of April 12th.  A strong upper level storm system approached the region from the west and the result was elevated strong to severe thunderstorms which produced mainly large hail across the region.  Later in the morning of Easter Sunday (04/12) a squall line developed across central and northern Texas and pushed into the region.  These storms produced large hail and strong straight line winds across the region.

A stationary front was located across the Mid-South during the evening hours of July 12th, 2009. A mesoscale convective complex developed along the front and moved into the area during the late evening hours. Showers and thunderstorms from the complex affected the Mid-South through the morning hours of July 13th, 2009. Some of the storms became severe and produced damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three inches of rain fell in Weiner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed over Mid-South during the late afternoon and evening hours of July 15th, 2009 due to daytime heating. The storms died out a couple hours after sunset. Some of the storms became severe with large hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds snapped the tops off of large trees in Oak Grove Heights.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms spread from northeast Oklahoma into northwest Arkansas during the afternoon of the 1st and lasted into the evening hours.

A strong but slow-moving low pressure system began its approach to Arkansas on the 23rd. Ahead of this low, unseasonably large amounts of moisture were drawn northward from the Gulf of Mexico. The approach of the low set off heavy rain and thunderstorms on the 23rd and 24th. Rainfall amounts of 3 to 6 inches were common for the two-day period. In a broad swath from southwest Arkansas through the central part of the state into northeast Arkansas, many places picked up 7 to 10 inches of rain. Flash flooding resulted initially, followed by areal flooding and river flooding. After the low passed by, colder air spilled into Arkansas. Light snow fell across parts of northern and western Arkansas from late on the 24th into the early morning hours of Christmas Day. Most snowfall amounts were in the 1/2 to 1 inch range, with spotty totals up to 2 inches. Though the amounts were rather small, hazardous driving conditions resulted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding, which developed early on the morning of the 24th, became widespread areal flooding later in the morning on the 24th and continued through Christmas. Arkansas Highways closed due to high water included:  14 just off U.S. Highway 67 near Newport to Waldenburg, 226 from U.S. Highway 67 to the Jackson/Craighead County line, 224 in the Tuckerman and Swifton areas, 18 Spur to Grubbs, 17 from Weldon to Tupelo, 42 south of Beedeville, and 18.  Jackson County Roads flooded included 42, 43, 81, 211, and 315.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well above normal rainfall from previous months, combined with additional heavy rainfall in early December and planned reservoir releases, caused the White and Cache rivers to rise above flood stage during the first part of the month. These rivers then remained above flood stage through the rest of December and into January, aided by additional very heavy rainfall on the 23rd and 24th. Thousands of acres of cropland and several rural roads became inundated due to the high water, causing additional damage to crops and roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Additional heavy rainfall at the beginning of December caused the Cache River at Patterson to again rise above flood stage during the afternoon of the 6th. The river remained above flood stage for two weeks before falling below flood stage on the afternoon of the 21st. This caused pasture and cropland along the river to become inundated once again.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure aloft approached from the west and collided with moisture and instability. Thunderstorms developed in eastern Oklahoma during the afternoon, then became more numerous as they entered western Arkansas during the evening. The storms brought a few tornadoes and numerous hailstorms to Arkansas during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado moved out of Howard County, and entered Pike County southwest of New Hope.  Hundreds of trees were knocked down or snapped off in the forest, primarily on private timber company land.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong but slow-moving low pressure system began its approach to Arkansas on the 23rd. Ahead of this low, unseasonably large amounts of moisture were drawn northward from the Gulf of Mexico. The approach of the low set off heavy rain and thunderstorms on the 23rd and 24th. Rainfall amounts of 3 to 6 inches were common for the two-day period. In a broad swath from southwest Arkansas through the central part of the state into northeast Arkansas, many places picked up 7 to 10 inches of rain. Flash flooding resulted initially, followed by areal flooding and river flooding. After the low passed by, colder air spilled into Arkansas. Light snow fell across parts of northern and western Arkansas from late on the 24th into the early morning hours of Christmas Day. Most snowfall amounts were in the 1/2 to 1 inch range, with spotty totals up to 2 inches. Though the amounts were rather small, hazardous driving conditions resulted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding, which developed on the evening of the 23rd, became widespread areal flooding on the 24th and continued through Christmas. Numerous county roads were flooded. In the Jacksonville area, four houses had to be evacuated just north of town, and residents of the Taramount neighborhood had to reach their homes by traveling in boats and then walking the rest of the way. The Jacksonville Fire Department rescued people from cars floating on West Main Street. On Arkansas Highway 161 South, a home and some mobile homes were flooded. Flooding also occurred on Jacksonville Cutoff, where a used car lot and a self-storage warehouse were flooded. Many homes along Valentine Road were flooded as well. In Sherwood, flooding occurred in Indianhead Lake Estates, around Bronco Lane, Windchime, Silvercreek, Orchid Lane, in Gravel Ridge, and on Trammel Road from Landers Road to Roundtop Road. Five homes were evacuated off Stagecoach Road in southwest Little Rock. Arkansas Highway 338 was closed in southwest Little Rock. Pinnacle Mountain State Park was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening of April 9th and overnight hours of the 10th, an outbreak of severe storms and tornadoes occurred across the ArkLaMiss region and pushed east through northern Louisiana and into northern Mississippi. While the majority of the severe weather occurred out of the NWS Jackson, MS service area, several reports of large hail and wind damage were reported along with a few tornadoes. One of the strongest storms moved east across Ashley County Arkansas. This storm produced an EF2 tornado over the southwest and southern sections, and contained a swath of quarter to golf ball sized hail which occurred across the entire length of the county.||In looking at this event, which occurred over two days (April 9-10), in a regional view, this was likely the biggest severe weather and tornado outbreak during the spring of 2009. This event contained numerous strong tornadoes along with hundreds of large hail reports which stretched from the Southern Plains to several other southern States.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado tracked across rural southern Ashley County and snapped and uprooted hundreds of large hardwood and softwood trees. Forty two homes were damaged with approximately 15 homes that were heavily damaged or destroyed. The most intense damage was along Meridian Church Road in southwest Ashley County. Here, intense tree damage resulted in several cross roads being blocked by dozens of trees, and several mobile homes and houses were destroyed by the winds and fallen trees. This was the area rated EF2 with 130 mph winds. Over the last several miles of the track, the tornado was narrower and damage was primarily in the EF1 category.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving line of thunderstorms, known as a derecho, rushed across Arkansas on the afternoon of the 12th. Widespread wind damage and one tornado resulted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A barn was destroyed and several farm buildings and barns were severely damaged.  Hundreds of trees were blown down, one of which sliced through a mobile home. Roofs of several residences were damaged. Power lines were blown down. Boats were blown around on a lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure aloft approached from the west and collided with moisture and instability. Thunderstorms developed in eastern Oklahoma during the afternoon, then became more numerous as they entered western Arkansas during the evening. The storms brought a few tornadoes and numerous hailstorms to Arkansas during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A roof was blown off a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the 29th and 30th, a front was nearly stationary near the Arkansas/Missouri border. Thunderstorms built into the western part of Arkansas on the afternoon and evening of the 29th. During the early morning hours of the 30th, a cluster of thunderstorms approached Arkansas from the west. Although these storms died out, they left behind an area of low pressure aloft. This low pressure then caused the development of new storms during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding occurred in the city of Waldron.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Active weather continued from May 8th through the 11th as another cold front arrived from the Plains and stalled in or near the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor tree damage occurred in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm, moist, unstable air mass was over Arkansas at the beginning of May. Areas of low pressure aloft moving across the state triggered scattered thunderstorms.||At North Little Rock and Morrilton, the rain contributed to making it the wettest May ever recorded in those cities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Arkansas Highway 124 was covered in water. Vehicles were unable to drive through.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms occurred during the evening hours across eastern Oklahoma. The storms spread into northwest Arkansas during the late evening hours of the 13th and early morning hours of the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated at 60 miles an hour blew down a large tree limb.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Excessive rainfall in May pushed the Arkansas River stage above flood stage at Pine Bluff.  Homes in the Island Harbour Subdivision were impacted by inundation or isolation by the Arkansas River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In a subdivision of more than 40 homes, high stages on the Arkansas River cut off access for most of the month.  There were two homes that received water damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving line of thunderstorms, known as a derecho, rushed across Arkansas on the afternoon of the 12th. Widespread wind damage and one tornado resulted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down, one of which fell on a house.  A highway sign was blown down on Interstate 40. The ceiling of the drive-though section of a car dealership was badly damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms that began in eastern Oklahoma spread into northwest and west central Arkansas during the midday hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 60 mile an hour wind gust from a thunderstorm was reported by a trained spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large storm system in the Rockies headed through the Plains into the upper Midwest on the 29th. Rain increased ahead of the system, and became widespread in Arkansas. Meanwhile, a cold front arrived from the west. Because the system was so far to the north, it did not give the front much push. The front slowed down, which prolonged the rain. The result was widespread flash flooding, which gave way to areal flooding and river flooding. Winds changed direction and speed up through the atmosphere, a favorable condition for tornadoes. However, instability was sufficient only in southern Arkansas for the development of tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Arkansas Highways 157, 124, 13, 16, 31, 36, and 87 were flooded, as were numerous county roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Before dawn on the 16th, scattered thunderstorms developed near the Missouri border and produced some severe weather. Severe storms were much more widespread across the southern and western counties later in the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down on Dallas County Road 103 at Jones Rogers Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving line of thunderstorms entered northern Arkansas during the wee hours of the 5th. Initially, mainly hail occurred.  However, as the line swept rapidly southward, wind damage became the main severe weather event, especially from central into eastern Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down in White Hall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon heating and lots of moisture was enough to trigger hit and miss showers and thunderstorms on the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A car stalled due to high water in Morgan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed across the region in association with a frontal system. Some of the storms brought hail, damaging winds, and flash flooding to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado siren and a control box were blown off of a pole.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system wobbled from eastern Texas into southwest Arkansas in mid-September. Precipitation became widespread just to the north and east of the system late on the 15th in west central, central and northeast sections of the state. There was tropical moisture in place, making showers and thunderstorms more efficient rain-makers. Heavy rain continued swirling around the system across Arkansas on the 16th through the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was deep in places around Augusta and McCrory.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large storm system brought heavy rain to Arkansas at the end of October, causing a number of river forecast points in the Little Rock Hydrologic Service Area to exceed flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall at the end of October pushed the White River at Batesville above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front moved through the region starting on the evening of October 29th and exited the region by late afternoon on October 30th. Numerous showers and thunderstorms produced flash flooding across portions of east Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Parts of State Highway 1 between Forrest City and Wynne were flooded. Six homes and one car dealership were flooded in that area. Several other homes west of Forrest City near County Road 226 had water approaching them. At least two people were evacuated from this area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large storm system in the Rockies headed through the Plains into the upper Midwest on the 29th. Rain increased ahead of the system, and became widespread in Arkansas. Meanwhile, a cold front arrived from the west. Because the system was so far to the north, it did not give the front much push. The front slowed down, which prolonged the rain. The result was widespread flash flooding, which gave way to areal flooding and river flooding. Winds changed direction and speed up through the atmosphere, a favorable condition for tornadoes. However, instability was sufficient only in southern Arkansas for the development of tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many trees were knocked down, with several of the trees falling on houses. Power lines were also felled.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periodic heavy rainfall during October caused a number of reporting stations in Arkansas to set new rainfall records for the month.  At three stations, October was the wettest month ever recorded.  At two stations, rainfall in October pushed the stations' yearly rainfall total so high such that the towns were assured that 2009 will be the wettest year ever recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Hot Springs Airport, it was the wettest October ever recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large storm system in the Rockies headed through the Plains into the upper Midwest on the 29th. Rain increased ahead of the system, and became widespread in Arkansas. Meanwhile, a cold front arrived from the west. Because the system was so far to the north, it did not give the front much push. The front slowed down, which prolonged the rain. The result was widespread flash flooding, which gave way to areal flooding and river flooding. Winds changed direction and speed up through the atmosphere, a favorable condition for tornadoes. However, instability was sufficient only in southern Arkansas for the development of tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding which began on the 29th became widespread areal flooding on the 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed from the central and southern Plains into the mid-Mississippi Valley early on the 8th. Rain eventually spread into Arkansas later in the day, and was heavy at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dozens of large trees and power lines were down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large storm system in the Rockies headed through the Plains into the upper Midwest on the 29th. Rain increased ahead of the system, and became widespread in Arkansas. Meanwhile, a cold front arrived from the west. Because the system was so far to the north, it did not give the front much push. The front slowed down, which prolonged the rain. The result was widespread flash flooding, which gave way to areal flooding and river flooding. Winds changed direction and speed up through the atmosphere, a favorable condition for tornadoes. However, instability was sufficient only in southern Arkansas for the development of tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of the 30th, a large cluster of thunderstorms known as a Mesoscale Convective System (MCS) moved into Arkansas from the southwest.   The MCS weakened, leaving behind a Mesoscale Convective Vortex (MCV) or small area of circulation aloft.  During the afternoon, this MCV helped to trigger new storms and a few tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: All of the damage from this tornado occurred on the farm at Cummins Prison, well away from the prison building itself.  A farm shop building had a metal door blown in and a metal wall peeled back.  Quite a few trees had large limbs torn off, and a few trees were uprooted altogether.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread severe thunderstorms moved into northwest and west central Arkansas from eastern Oklahoma during the early evening hours of the 9th.

Low pressure aloft approached from the west and collided with moisture and instability. Thunderstorms developed in eastern Oklahoma during the afternoon, then became more numerous as they entered western Arkansas during the evening. The storms brought a few tornadoes and numerous hailstorms to Arkansas during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down on a roof on Weddingford Road. A pine tree fell in front of a home on West Searcy. A tree was down on the roof of a carport on West Walnut. Several trees were down across the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Much above normal rainfall occurred in May, which led to river flooding along the White and Black Rivers.  Crop losses were reported by farmers in eastern Arkansas.||Clarendon set a record for the wettest May ever recorded in the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High stages on the White River caused crop damages.  The White River stage at Augusta remained above flood stage all month.  The White River stage at Georgetown remained above flood stage from May 7th to 19th.  Young soybean crops were destroyed and new plantings delayed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A round of excessive rain occurred on the 12th as a warm front lifted into the state from the Gulf Coast. Yet another cold front was poised to the north during the afternoon of the 13th. Strong to severe storms worked into northern and central Arkansas during the overnight hours early on the 14th. High pressure built over the region, and brought dry and warm weather through the 15th. The high exited to the west by the 16th, and created a northwest wind flow aloft. This drove a new cold front and scattered thunderstorms into the state.

A storm system in the Plains spread showers into Arkansas on the 9th. It was windy, with southerly winds averaging 15 to 25 mph (30 to 40 mph gusts). During the overnight hours of the 10th and the wee hours of the 11th, severe weather become a concern in Arkansas. The new system dragged a cold front into the state, with a line of fast moving thunderstorms along the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down near Ludwig.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A round of excessive rain occurred on the 12th as a warm front lifted into the state from the Gulf Coast. Yet another cold front was poised to the north during the afternoon of the 13th. Strong to severe storms worked into northern and central Arkansas during the overnight hours early on the 14th. High pressure built over the region, and brought dry and warm weather through the 15th. The high exited to the west by the 16th, and created a northwest wind flow aloft. This drove a new cold front and scattered thunderstorms into the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down on U.S. Highway 412.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unstable air, fronts meandering in the state, and areas of low pressure aloft contributed to severe weather, including a few tornadoes.||At Arkansas Post (Arkansas County), the rain contributed to making it the wettest May ever recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred in the area. In Pine Bluff, eight homes on Hagan Drive as well as an apartment complex on 5th and S. Palm St. had damage due to flooding. Water also entered some homes in Wabbaseka and Altheimer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving line of thunderstorms, known as a derecho, rushed across Arkansas on the afternoon of the 12th. Widespread wind damage and one tornado resulted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread tree damaged occurred, and about 30 homes were damaged by the falling trees. A wall of a food plant was blown down, and a rail station at another business was damaged. Several other businesses suffered damage, as well. Many power lines were blown down. A pavilion at a church collapsed. Two chicken houses were badly damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving line of thunderstorms, known as a derecho, rushed across Arkansas on the afternoon of the 12th. Widespread wind damage and one tornado resulted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell on a boat.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure aloft approached from the west and collided with moisture and instability. Thunderstorms developed in eastern Oklahoma during the afternoon, then became more numerous as they entered western Arkansas during the evening. The storms brought a few tornadoes and numerous hailstorms to Arkansas during the evening hours.

Low pressure aloft approached from the west and collided with moisture and instability. Thunderstorms developed in eastern Oklahoma during the afternoon, then became more numerous as they entered western Arkansas during the evening. The storms brought a few tornadoes and numerous hailstorms to Arkansas during the evening hours.

Low pressure aloft approached from the west and collided with moisture and instability. Thunderstorms developed in eastern Oklahoma during the afternoon, then became more numerous as they entered western Arkansas during the evening. The storms brought a few tornadoes and numerous hailstorms to Arkansas during the evening hours.

A strong cold front moved through the Midsouth during the late evening of April 9, 2009 into the afternoon of April 10, 2009. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the front during the evening hours and moved into the Midsouth before weakening during the early morning hours. Some of these thunderstorms became severe and produced large hail and damaging winds. By mid-morning, redevelopment of thunderstorms occurred along the front. These thunderstorms became severe and produced large hail as well.

Low pressure aloft approached from the west and collided with moisture and instability. Thunderstorms developed in eastern Oklahoma during the afternoon, then became more numerous as they entered western Arkansas during the evening. The storms brought a few tornadoes and numerous hailstorms to Arkansas during the evening hours.

A moist, semi-tropical atmosphere existed over Arkansas on the 21st.  A cold front moving through the state triggered some showers.  A waterspout and two tornadoes occurred from mid afternoon through early evening.  These occurred without any thunder, indicating that the mechanism that caused them was much like the one that triggers waterspouts off the Florida Keys in the summer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roof damage occurred to a couple of houses and a number of trees were blown down or snapped off.  Yard items were blown around, including a grill, a small flat-bottom boat, and a deer feeder.  A fence was blown down.
